title: health check: add on-failure actions
body:
For systems that have extreme robustness requirements (edge devices, particularly those in difficult to access environments), it is important that applications continue running in all circumstances. When the application fails, Podman must restart it automatically to provide this robustness. Otherwise, these devices may require customer IT to physically gain access to restart, which can be prohibitively difficult.

Add a new `--on-failure` flag that supports four actions:

- **none**: Take no action.

- **kill**: Kill the container.

- **restart**: Restart the container.  Do not combine the `restart` action with the `--restart` flag.  When running inside of a systemd unit, consider using the `kill` or `stop` action instead to make use of systemd's restart policy.

- **stop**: Stop the container.

To remain backwards compatible, **none** is the default action.

Signed-off-by: Valentin Rothberg <vrothberg@redhat.com>

<!--
Thanks for sending a pull request!

Please make sure you've read our contributing guidelines and how to submit a pull request (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#submitting-pull-requests).

In case you're only changing docs, make sure to prefix the pull-request title with "[CI:DOCS]". That will prevent functional tests from running and save time and energy.

Finally, be sure to sign commits with your real name. Since by opening
a PR you already have commits, you can add signatures if needed with
something like `git commit -s --amend`.
-->

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

<!--
If no, just write `None` in the release-note block below. If yes, a release note
is required: Enter your extended release note in the block below. If the PR
requires additional action from users switching to the new release, include the

For more information on release notes, please follow the Kubernetes model:
https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/release-notes.md
-->

```release-note
Add a new `--health-on-failure` flag to specify an action to take once the container turns unhealthy.
```

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *vrothberg* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)~~ [vrothberg] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

Nice work
LGTM
But what about Pods and `podman kube play`

> But what about Pods and `podman kube play`

Those can, in theory, be paired as well.  Podman already supports restart policies for Kube _but_ health checks are not wired into it. Definitely something we should consider tackling in the near future.

> [+1061s] # #|         > 'time="2022-09-08T05:39:35-05:00" level=error msg="Failed to start transient timer unit: Unit c26ee584b585cd1078cc26692d3f905006b65bda1ec1fc6e2fb19c5218a1d595.timer already exists.\n"'

That should probably be a debug log only.
Why "do not combine the restart action with the `--restart` flag"? I think that's probably acceptable.

> Why "do not combine the restart action with the `--restart` flag"? I think that's probably acceptable.

They would probably compete.  I think `--restart=on-failure` and `--health-on-failure=kill` would make more sense.
Thanks for the reviews.  Updated accordingly, most notably the plumbing for `podman inspect` is done and tested.

> Tests are very unhappy, differences in exited/stopped and running/stopping, possibly race conditions?

Looks like the containers did not transition to the final state yet.  It makes sense given the cleanup process collects and records the exit code.  It's probably best to check for `!= "running"`.  Thanks for the other comments.
